coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived nitrogen-doped water soluble carbon dots from Prosopis juliflora for Fe3+ sensing

coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots have gained intensive interest in fluorescence-based bioimaging and metal-ion sensing applications, due to their approach being simple, safe, eco-friendly, with good biocompatibility, and high fluorescence stability, especially when derived from greener renewable plant materials. This study reports the green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using Prosopis juliflora plant stems under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized NCDs were carefully studied to understand their structural, optical, and morphological properties. The NCDs displayed blue emission under 365 nm with excellent stability and high water solubility. The fluorescent quantum yield was found to be ∼ 4.92 %. The HRTEM images revealed spherical particles with an average particle size of ∼ 8.6 nm. The synthesized NCDs were studied for sensing Fe(III) ions exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity for Fe3+ ions, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.593 μM with a linear range of 20 – 200 µM. Apart from sensing ferric ions, the synthesized NCDs can also be used to determine the pH of the sample. The NCDs showed a pH-dependent fluorescent emission producing linearity from pH 2 to 9. Furthermore, Fe3+ ion sensing was conducted using real samples, including groundwater and lake water. This work demonstrates the green synthesis of NCDs as an eco-friendly material for efficient sensing of Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived nitrogen-doped water soluble carbon dots from Prosopis juliflora for Fe3+ sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots have gained intensive interest in fluorescence-based bioimaging and metal-ion sensing applications, due to their approach being simple, safe, eco-friendly, with good biocompatibility, and high fluorescence stability, especially when derived from greener renewable plant materials. This study reports the green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using Prosopis juliflora plant stems under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized NCDs were carefully studied to understand their structural, optical, and morphological properties. The NCDs displayed blue emission under 365 nm with excellent stability and high water solubility. The fluorescent quantum yield was found to be ∼ 4.92 %. The HRTEM images revealed spherical particles with an average particle size of ∼ 8.6 nm. The synthesized NCDs were studied for sensing Fe(III) ions exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity for Fe3+ ions, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.593 μM with a linear range of 20 – 200 µM. Apart from sensing ferric ions, the synthesized NCDs can also be used to determine the pH of the sample. The NCDs showed a pH-dependent fluorescent emission producing linearity from pH 2 to 9. Furthermore, Fe3+ ion sensing was conducted using real samples, including groundwater and lake water. This work demonstrates the green synthesis of NCDs as an eco-friendly material for efficient sensing of Fe3+ ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were used in several biomedical applications for their benefits such as minimal toxicity, strong biocompatibility, chemical resistance, photostability, and good water solubility [1]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a particle size smaller than 10 nm. Compared to semiconductor quantum dots [2], CQDs have incredibly low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility, and hence were used in several applications, including photocatalysis [345], bioimaging [67], wastewater treatment, hydrogen production [8], fluorescence sensing [9], cancer therapy [1011], drug administration [12], optoelectronic devices, and cellular labeling [13].

CDs can be synthesized using several synthesis methods [1415] in which carbonization is one of the simple methods involving heat treatment of biomass to synthesize CDs [16]. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a typical biomass refining process utilized to produce CDs from biomass with large cellulose content [1,17]. Different sources of biomass have been used to make CDs namely leaves [18,19], wood [1], fruit juice [20,21], mushrooms [10], papaya seeds [22], beetroot [23] and fruit peels [3]. Apart from using the as-synthesized CDs prepared from bio-sources, there is research concerning the doping of CDs with one or more metals or non-metals. This enhances the optical properties, biocompatibility, and physicochemical properties [2425].

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is an important transition metal ion in both biological and ecological systems. It carries out a vital role in various essential functions, especially in the construction of myoglobin and hemoglobin, as well as in several enzymatic processes. Furthermore, iron is indispensable for critical biological activities such as enzymatic catalysis, nucleic acid synthesis, and cellular metabolism in addition to oxygen and electron transport. Its presence is essential for sustaining life and supporting various vital processes within organisms. Imbalances in the levels of Fe3+ ions can give rise to various disorders and diseases [26]. Excessive Fe3+ ions can lead to a condition called hemochromatosis, characterized by excessive accumulation of iron in the body and this can result in the impairment of organ functions, including heart, lungs, and pancreas. Conversely, a deficiency of Fe3+ ions can cause anemia, a condition categorized by a decrease in red blood cells or insufficient hemoglobin levels [27]. Hence, it is crucial to detect and monitor the Fe3+ levels for the benefit of both human health and the environment to ensure well-being. The detection of Fe3+ ions has been carried out using analytical techniques like atomic absorption spectrometry, voltammetry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and electrochemical methods. These methods though provide precise and sensitive measurements of Fe3+ ion concentration, but their incurred high cost forbids them from real application in scientific research and environmental monitoring [28].

CDs-based nanomaterials have gained significant attention as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions attributed to their low cost, high sensitivity, straightforward operation, excellent reproducibility, and offers to detect lower Fe concentrations with high reliability [28,29] than other nanomaterials used for Fe3+ ion detection [3031]. CDs are now used as a substitute for the traditional fluorescence probe-based detection of metal ions, due to the distinctive optical properties like unique fluorescence characteristics, tunable emission wavelengths, high quantum yields, and excellent photostability of CDs. The versatility and sensitivity of CDs have led to their increasing popularity as a viable option for detecting and quantifying metal ions in various analytical and sensing applications. Depending on the structural, optical, and physicochemical properties CDs are used as probes for metal sensing [3233]. Various mechanism is employed for the detection of metal ions such as complex formation, inner filter effect (IFE), FRET (Forster Resonance Energy Transfer), ion binding, aggregation, and PET-based quenching [34].

Oxidative stress in humans is a serious concern when an imbalance between the cell's antioxidant defense system and oxidants occurs, under the presence of excess oxidants. This imbalance causes oxidative modifications at the molecular level, affecting components such as lipids, proteins, DNA, and more, which contain unsaturated bonds. These oxidative modifications can lead to cellular damage and, ultimately, an accelerated process of cellular death [35]. CDs have shown great antioxidant properties which provide an added benefit [36].

Prosopis juliflora is an invasive plant species present in various parts of the world, that deteriorates the growth of native plants and negatively affects the biodiversity in the area. Reports have shown that the levels of groundwater and soil nutrients have drastically reduced due to Prosopis juliflora plants [37,38]. Prosopis juliflora has a large content of polysaccharides of holocellulose, α-cellulose, lignin, and pentosans [39], which can be a great source of carbon and nitrogen for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Statistical analysis has been carried out regarding the management costs for the removal of invasive species in India [40]. Owing to its environmental effects that threaten biodiversity, the removal of Prosopis juliflora species is being carried out. The use of Prosopis juliflora waste effectively enables salvage of the cost incurred. When employed for large-scale production and material processing, the effective cost is much lower due to the use of biomass waste as a carbon source, reducing material costs compared to the use of other sources such as organic compounds or biomolecules [41].

In this study, green synthesis of NCDs using Prosopis juliflora plant stems with intense blue emission under 365 nm is reported as a fluorescent probe for the sensing of Fe3+ ions. The synthesized NCDs have an average particle size of ∼ 8.6 nm with a narrow particle size distribution (5–12 nm). Furthermore, the NCDs showed a quantum yield of ∼ 4.92 % with excellent photostability, retaining fluorescence intensity under prolonged excitation and high water-solubility. The high tolerance of NCDs to changes in ionic strength makes them suitable for various aqueous environment-based applications. The synthesized NCDs showed great sensing activity for the Fe3+ ions. In addition, the NCDs can also be used for pH sensing in solutions of both acidic and basic conditions with an excellent linearity in the pH range of 2 to 9.

The Prosopis juliflora stems were washed several times using deionized water, dried at 100 °C for 24 h, and powdered. The powdered sample was sieved through a 100-mesh sieve and stored in a dry place at room temperature (RT; 25 °C). Quinine sulphate, H2SO4 (98 %), HCl (37 %), NaOH, CuCl2·2H2O (99 %), FeCl3·6H2O (97 %), FeCl2·4H2O (98 %), Pb(NO3)2 (99 %), HgCl2 (98 %),), CrCl3·6H2O (98 %), NaCl (99.9 %), AlCl3·6H2O (99 %), CoCl2·6H2O (99 %), MnCl2·4H2O (99 %), BaCl2·2H2O (99 %), Ca(NO3)2·4H2O (99 %), were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory (SRL), India. KCl, MgCl2·6H2O (99.5 %) was purchased from HiMedia. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4), and Na2HAsO4·7H2O (98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. All the chemicals were used without any further purification and DI water was used for all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs from Prosopis juliflora
ce:section.ce:para: The powdered extract of Prosopis juliflora was mixed with 3 % (v/v) acetic acid solution and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. To remove unreacted moieties, the resultant product is sonicated to obtain a homogeneous mixture and centrifuged at 8000 rpm. The solution is filtered using a 0.22 µm filter to separate the NCDs and dialyzed with 1KDa membrane to remove water-soluble impurities. Finally, the attained NCD solution is stored at 4 °C and used for characterizations. Fig. S1 represents the synthesis procedure of NCDs from Prosopis juliflora stems.

UV–Visible spectra were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, UV-VIS, Lambda 650) in the wavelength range from 200 to 800 nm. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using Bruker FT-IR spectrometer (Given more details like model). Raman spectroscopy was done using Horiba, XploRATM Plus Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm laser in the range of 1000 to 3000 cm−1. The excitation and emission spectrum were measured using a fluorescent spectrometer (Horiba, FluoroMax plus), and fluorescence lifetime studies were performed using Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (Horiba, DeltaTime TCSPC Lifetime kit). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was determined using PANanalytical instrument (Netherlands) at a scan rate of 10◦/min in the 2θ range of 10° to 80° with CuKα1 radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using PHI − VERSAPROBE III – XPS with a monochromatic X-ray Beam (15 µm). High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN [FEI] with a 200 KV microscope for the microscopic analysis of synthesized NCDs to determine the morphology, particle size, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern.

The fluorescent quantum yield of NCDs was calculated by relative method, with Quinine sulphate dissolved in 0.1 N H2SO4 as the standard reference. The optical absorbance of both reference and samples was kept below 0.05 and the emission spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The quantum yield of the NCDs was calculated using the Eq. (1).
(1)

where φS and φR are the quantum yields of quinine sulphate and NCDs, respectively; η represents the refractive index of the solvent, IR and IS are the measured integrated emission intensity and AR and AS are the optical densities of quinine sulphate (R) and NCDs (S) respectively. For an aqueous solution, ηS/ηR = 1, φR = 0.54.

The stability of fluorescent emission of the NCDs was studied under different conditions such as ionic strength and UV exposure. The stability of NCDs to ionic strength is determined using NaCl and KCl solutions. In the presence of NaCl, the experiment is achieved by adding 100 µg of NCDs into 3 mL of NaCl solution of varying molar concentration. A similar procedure is followed with the addition of varying molar concentrations of KCl solutions. The stability of NCDs under UV light is done by placing the NCDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp. The NCDs solution is collected at different intervals of time, its emission intensity is recorded and the stability experiments were carried out in triplicates to test the repeatability.

The fluorescence quenching of NCDs by Fe3+ ions was investigated at room temperature. The quenching was studied with different concentrations of NCDs and then for different duration of interactions. The detection of Fe3+ ions was executed by adding 2 mL of various concentrations of Fe3+, into 1 mL of NCDs (0.1 mg/mL). The emission spectra of the solutions were then measured for an excitation of 400 nm after the reaction was kept for 1 min. Different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (20–300 μM) were added to the NCDs solution and the emission spectral intensity at each concentration of Fe3+ ions was measured. Selectivity and specificity of NCDs for Fe3+ ions sensing were examined by mixing it with other interfering metal ions namely Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, HAsO4 2−, Ba2+, Cr2+, Fe2+, K+, and Fe3+ and the emission intensities were recorded for an excitation of 400 nm.

The groundwater sample was collected to test the performance of the sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions as real samples. The sensor was developed as an alternative for the detection of small doses of Fe3+ ions present in drinking water. For this, groundwater and lake water (Bahour Lake) were collected, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and further filtered using a 0.22-µm filter to remove impurities. Finally, the water samples were 10-fold diluted. In 2 mL of aqueous real sample, 1 mL of NCDs and Fe3+ ions were added, and the PL emission intensities were recorded at 400 nm.

A stock solution of different pH is prepared using HCl and NaOH. The experiment is carried out by adding 100 µg of NCDs into stock solutions of varying pH (pH = 2 to 9) in both acidic and basic regions. The NCDs solution was excited at 400 nm and the emission spectra were recorded.

The antioxidant property of NCDs was studied by colorimetric assay based on the discoloration of the oxidized form of DPPH from purple to its reduced form in yellow. The higher the antioxidants, the yellower is the color of the final solution. Typically, in 0.1 mM of DPPH solution, varying concentrations of NCDs (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µM) were added and made up to 3 mL. These solutions are kept in the dark for 30 min and subsequently optical absorbance of the sample is recorded at 517 nm. The percentage of free radical scavenging activity using DPPH is calculated using Eq. (2)
(2)

The synthesis of NCDs was done using a simple hydrothermal technique and the shape and size of NCD particles were studied by HRTEM. Fig. 1 a shows spherical NCD particles with an average particle size of 8.6 nm and monodispersing in nature. The particle size distribution ranges from 5 to 12 nm as shown in Fig. 1c. The calculated d-spacings of 0.43 and 0.23 nm match with the XRD pattern [42]. The slightly larger particle size distribution may be due to the nature of the sample and the duration of hydrothermal carbonization [43].

The broad XRD peak of the as-synthesized NCDs suggests the amorphous nature of NCDs with mild crystallinity. The peaks at 26° and 46° correspond to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively (Fig. 1d) that match well with the JCPDS card no. 89–8487 [44]. The d-spacing is calculated using Bragg Eq. (3),
(3)
n λ = 2 d s i n θ
where λ is the wavelength of the X-rays, θ is the angle of incidence and reflection, n is an integer, and d is the distance between atoms.

The Raman spectrum was performed to distinguish the sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms of the NCDs. The presence of bands at 1329 and 1546 cm−1 corresponds to the D band of sp3-hybridized carbon and the G band of sp2-hybridized carbon (Fig. 2 a). The D-band is related to the disorder and originates from carbon with structural defects, while the G-band comes from well-ordered carbon clusters with sp2 hybridization [45]. The relative intensity ratio of D and G bands denoted by ID/IG , provides a potential method for evaluating the degree of graphitization (graphitic ordering) in the synthesized NCDs and was recognized as a parameter for assessing disorder and crystallinity [46,29]. The ID/IG value of 1.01 signifies the occurrence of structural defects within the NCDs [46] and is evident that NCDs consist of mild crystalline graphitic structure [47]. These results match well with the HRTEM and SAED results.

Further, the Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed to assess the surface functional groups of the sample. The broad band from 3785 to 2830 cm−1 with a peak centered at 3435 cm−1 corresponds to OH or N-H stretching vibrations. The hydroxyl and amine groups are formed during hydrothermal synthesis in the presence of water molecules and amine as amino acids from the precursors [48]. The small band in the range from 2380 to 1895 cm−1 with a peak centered at 2071 cm−1 corresponds to C≡C stretching vibration. The sharp peak centered at 1638 cm−1 corresponds to C=C/COOH stretching vibrations. These functional groups were formed mainly due to the cellulose and hemicellulose presence in the precursor [39]. The minor peaks at 1339, 1046, and 670 cm−1 belong to the –COOH symmetric stretching vibration, C-N stretching vibration of amine or C-O vibrations of alcohol, and C=C bending of alkene, respectively. This may be due to the presence of acetic acid and water in the precursors during hydrothermal carbonization. The carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups of NCDs provide excellent aqueous solubility and are suitable for biological applications.

XPS was measured to further investigate the surface functionalities and elemental composition of NCDs. The XPS survey spectrum exhibited three distinct peaks at ∼ 286, 401, and 533 eV indicating the presence of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s), respectively as shown in Fig. 3 a and offering insights into their surface states. The composition of the NCDs revealed that carbon constituted 75.3 %, nitrogen accounted for 1.3 %, and oxygen made up 23.4 %. These results demonstrate the presence of nitrogen in the synthesized NCDs.

The C 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3b) is resolved into three different peaks centered at 284.31, 285.28, and 287.97 eV attributed to C-C(sp2)/C=C of the graphitic carbon, C-N/C-O, and C=O species, respectively [45,49]. The O 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed two peaks at 530.9 and 532.47 eV attributed to the C=O and C-O species, respectively [50]. The deconvolution of the N 1 s peak (Fig. 3d) resulted in two peaks at 401.87 and 402.76 eV assigned to the graphitic-N and nitrogen oxides of the O=N-C species, respectively [51]. These species match well with the functional groups identified from FTIR analysis.

The optical properties were analyzed using UV–Visible absorption studies. The NCD sample exhibited an absorption band (Fig. 4 a) ranging from 250 to 350 nm. The absorption peak at 280 nm corresponds to the π-π* energy transition of the conjugated C=C bonds and confirms the existence of an aromatic π-system in the NCDs [52]. The NCDs solution was pale yellow under daylight and exhibit bright blue emission under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 4a). The bandgap of the NCDs solution was calculated from a Tauc plot using absorption spectra and was found to be 3.62 eV (indirect bandgap) as shown in Fig. S2.

The fluorescent property of the sample was investigated using a spectrofluorometer. An intense emission spectrum was observed with peak maxima at ∼ 470 nm for an excitation of 400 nm (Fig. 4c). The emission spectral intensity usually depends on the surface sites (surface defects) and band gap transition arising from the surface functional groups on the NCDs as reported earlier. A Stokes shift of 1171 meV was observed (Fig. 4b), which denotes that the emission may arise from the electron-hole recombination through defect sites since the value of the Stokes shift is large [20]. For excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 420 nm, the emission spectra were measured and were found to show an excitation-dependent emission wavelength, that agrees with the typical emission properties of carbon nanomaterials [53](Fig. 4b). The emission spectra show typical red-shifted emission wavelengths from 447 to 520 nm for excitation from 300 to 420 nm, respectively with a wavelength increment of 20 nm. The emission intensity increased for the excitation from 300 to 400 nm and reached maximum emission at an excitation of 400 nm. After which the intensity began to drop at 420 nm. These results confirmed the excitation-dependent emission wavelengths feature of NCDs. The normalized emission spectra denote the red-shifted emission features with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4d). This red-shifted emission is proposed to occur by multiple mechanisms such as quantum confinement, surface state (surface functional group), synergistic effect of surface state, molecular fluorescence, and non-uniform particle size of CDs [52,53].

The quantum yield of as-synthesized NCDs was calculated using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and monitoring emission at 450 nm, using quinine sulphate as the standard. A quantum yield of ∼ 4.92 % was obtained for NCDs showing a reasonable quantum yield comparable with the earlier reports [18,47,54]. The mechanism of fluorescence and high quantum yield can depend on structure, carbonization behavior, and surface states. The amino acid residue from the precursor and N-doping contributes to the high quantum yield of NCDs [48]. Another common mechanism is the band gap transition of the π-domains and surface defects [55]. The surface defects are addressed using Raman spectra, where G-band intensity is attributed to disorder and can be correlated to the large stoke shift of the sample. These defects act as sites for band transitions and fluorescence.

The surface charge plays a vital role in the sensing mechanism of the NCDs. The zeta potential of the NCDs was studied and determined to have a high negative potential of −39.2 mV (Fig. 5 a). A high negative zeta potential value suggests that NCDs possess carboxyl and hydroxyl surface functional groups, which is consistent with FTIR and XPS results [50]. This suggests that the highly negatively charged groups on the surface of NCDs would impart higher stability and high dispersibility [56]. A zeta potential value of more than ± 30 mV indicates good colloidal stability against aggregation due to electrostatic repulsion between the NCD particles [57]. The hydrodynamic radius of the NCDs was determined using DLS analysis and shown to have monodispersed particles with a diameter of 171 nm as shown in Fig. 5b. The particle size obtained from DLS is larger than the average particle size from HR-TEM images, which is due to the presence of various functional groups on the surface of the NCDs.

The photoluminescence (PL) stability of NCDs was tested in the presence of various salt (NaCl and KCl) concentrations and under UV exposure. Moreover, the effect of ionic strength on the PL intensity showed no significant change with increasing concentrations of NaCl and KCl of up to 2 M, as shown in Fig. 5c. This demonstrates the exceptional stability of NCDs in high salt concentrations. The PL intensity exhibited minimal effects under continuous illumination of UV light, retaining about 89 % of the original emission intensity even after 90 min., suggesting excellent resistance of NCDs to photobleaching (Fig. 5d). This may be due to the particle size, nitrogen content, and surface functional groups of NCDs. The high photostability of NCDs could facilitate their application for sensing, bioimaging, and biolabeling applications [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of NCDs for Fe3+-ion sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL response of NCDs in the presence of several metal ions such as Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, HAsO4 2−, Ba2+, Cr2+, Fe2+, K+, and Fe3+ were performed to assess the sensing property and selectivity to different metal ions. The change in the PL emission intensity (Fo/F) at 462 nm was observed with the metal ion concentration of 200 µM. The F and F0
values are the PL intensity of NCDs with and without metal ions. The histogram plot using Fo/F versus similar concentration (200 µM) of metal ions was plotted and shown in Fig. 6 a.

The sensing ability and sensitivity were evaluated by recording the PL intensity of NCDs and the change in PL intensity after the addition of several metal ions. The Fe3+ ions exhibited a significant quenching of PL intensity than other metal ions. The PL intensity was quenched to ∼ 44 % (Fo/F = 2.16) when 200 µM of Fe3+ ions were added to NCDs. In the case of other metal ions, the PL intensity, either slightly changed or not changed was observed revealing negligible effects. Other metal ions, namely Cu2+ and Fe2+ showed minimal PL quenching (Fo/F = 1.1 and 1.13) probably due to the Metal2+/NCDs complex formation but were insignificant. The response to Fe2+ was minimum (Fo/F = 1.13) suggesting that the NCDs specifically have great response to Fe3+ ions, further indicating great selectivity. These results suggest that the NCDs have a great selectivity for Fe3+ ion sensing due to their stronger interaction between them.

The effect of NCD concentration on the quenching efficiency of Fe3+ ions was evaluated for the concentrations of 1, 0.1, and 0.01 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 6b, the F0/F response for the 0.1 and 1 mg/mL are 2.17 and 2.38, respectively. These concentrations show very little difference in response. Hence, 0.1 mg/mL was selected since a lower concentration was preferred and is utilized for further studies.

The PL response to Fe3+ ions was recorded at different time intervals. Specifically, the PL emission intensity of NCDs was recorded immediately (5 sec) after the addition of Fe3+ ions, and then after 1, 5, 10, 20, and 30 min intervals. From Fig. 6c, compared to the blank sample, the PL response for the test sample was taken immediately with F0/F = 2.17, whereas as the time for interaction increased, the response slightly increased with F0/F = 2.59 for 5 min of interaction. After this, the PL response dropped drastically at 10 min (F0/F = 1.98) and showed a slight increase as the time increased. This is due to the mechanism of quenching of Fe3+ ions due to the complex formation which is a weak interaction and is susceptible to dissociation over time [58]. The time for interaction of 5 sec i.e. immediately was taken for further study. Although the 5-minute interval had a slightly higher response, the difference was very small and the interaction time of 5 s was picked for the convenience of the experiment, a quick response being more suitable for practical real-time applications.

Further, the sensitivity of NCDs for the sensing of Fe3+ ions was tested by adding varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions (20 to 300 µM) and monitoring the PL emission intensity. The NCDs without the addition of Fe3+ ions showed emission at 460 nm under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The PL intensity quenched consistently to about 56 % of its initial intensity with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 6d). This result reveals the high sensitivity of NCDs to varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions, which tend to be effectively complex with the NCDs.

The plot between Fo/F and different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (20 to 300 µM) showed an excellent linear relationship in the range of 20 – 200 µM with the squared correlation coefficient of R 2 = 0.996 (Fig. 7 a, b). It is observed that the increase in Fe3+ concentration corresponds to the decrease in PL intensity. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the Eq. (4),
(4) LOD = 3σ/s
where σ is the standard deviation of blank (n = 3) and s is the slope of the linear curve. The LOD of the Fe3+ ion was about 0.593 μM demonstrating high sensitivity of NCDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions. The equation for the linear relationship (y = 0.00329x + 0.3189) between NCDs quenching (F0/F) and concentration of Fe3+ ions.

To assess the reliability of the NCDs-based sensor in real samples the performance was evaluated using groundwater. Accordingly, for the groundwater sample, the linear relationship (y = 0.00402x + 0.99603) between NCDs quenching (F0/F) and concentration of Fe3+ ions was obtained (Fig. S3a).

The Fe3+ ions spiked samples were also determined using the same procedure as previously done and calculated using the standard calibration curve (Fig. S3b). The performance detection of Fe3+ ions (n = 3) was carried out for spiked samples using the same procedure as above. The Fe3+ in the water sample was determined through a regression equation of the standard calibration curve. Recovery of known spiked samples was between 92.8 and 102.2 %. The RSD% was also below 6 % shown in Table 1 . Similarly, the linear relationship for the lake water sample is y = 0.0046x + 1.02566 (Fig. S4a,b). Recovery of known spiked samples was between 94.6 and 101.5 % showing great accuracy and the RSD% was also below 8 %.

The sensing efficiency of other reported CDs for Fe3+ ions is tabulated in Table 2 , along with a comparison of quantum yield, linear range of detection, and LOD of Fe3+ ions with Prosopis juliflora-derived NCDs. This result suggests higher selectivity and sensitivity of the developed NCDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions, with a low detection limit.

There are different mechanisms of quenching such as the inner filter effect (IFE), chelation, and complex formation. IFE occurs when the quencher i.e. Fe3+ ion absorption overlaps with the excitation or emission spectra of NCDs [34]. The absorption peak of ferric chloride is at 300 nm, whereas the excitation and emission peaks of NCDs are 400 nm and 470 nm respectively [62]. Hence, there is negligible overlap of peaks indicating that IFE is not responsible for the quenching. The next possible mechanism is the chelation of Fe3+ with the NCDs sample. Fig. 7c shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of NCDs in the presence of 0 and 200 µM of Fe3+ along with the absorption of Fe3+ ion solution without NCDs for comparison. From these spectra, it can be seen that the absorbance band at 280 nm due to the presence of Fe3+ is enhanced with the addition of metal ions. This is attributed to the formation of a complex chelate by the binding of Fe3+ with the nitrogen site of the NCDs as well as the binding with hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the NCDs sample [6364]. The mechanism of quenching was further studied by analyzing the fluorescent lifetime measurement of NCDs with and without metal ions.

The fluorescent lifetime of NCDs was analyzed using the TCSPC method. The decay curve was tri-exponentially fitted, and the average lifetime was calculated using Eq. (5).
(5)
τ
av
=
τ 1
B 1
+
τ 2
B 2
+
τ 3
B 3
B 1
×
B 2
×
B 3
where τ av is the average lifetime of the sample, τ 1, τ 2, τ 3 are the tri-exponential function, and B1, B2, B3 are the relative amplitude.

From Fig. 7d, it can be seen that the lifetime of NCDs was not altered much even in the presence of Fe3+ ions. From the fluorescent decay curve, the average lifetime of NCDs with and without the presence of Fe3+ metal ions was found to be 4.31 ns and 4.64 ns, respectively. Hence, it is evident that the quenching mechanism is static, suggesting the complex formation between the Fe3+ ions and the NCDs in the ground state that leads to the quenching phenomenon [50]. Since the quenching is static and not dynamic, FRET-based sensing can be ruled out which leads to complex formation and chelation. This results in the transfer of charges between NCDs and the metal ions through non-radiative recombination leading to quenching [65]. The effect of pH on the fluorescent emission of NCDs was analyzed. As shown in Fig. S5a the emission intensity varies with change in pH value. The NCDs sample has shown great response and linearity for a pH range of 2 to 9 and a correlation coefficient of 0.967.

DPPH is a stable free radical with a purple color that interacts with hydrogen donors. The molecule of DPPH possesses delocalized electrons throughout, preventing dimerization and giving it its distinctive color. Upon reaction with an antioxidant, the DPPH radical is reduced to its hydrazine form, resulting in a color shift from purple to pale yellow. The intensity of the color change, indicating the reduction of the DPPH radical, depends on the concentration of the antioxidant being tested. The greater the concentration of the antioxidant, the more pronounced the reduction of the DPPH radical and the corresponding color change. The antioxidant scavenging activity of the NCDs is determined using the DPPH assay [35]. Fig. S5b shows the bar chart of the antioxidant scavenging activity of NCDs in the concentration range from 20–100 µg/mL. The antioxidant activity of the NCDs was analyzed using DPPH assay. The as-synthesized NCDs showed great antioxidant properties and showed ∼93.59 % radical scavenging activity (RSA) for 100 µg of NCDs added showing these NCDs may be considered for bio-applications.

In this study, green synthesis of NCDs was developed through an easy and simple hydrothermal method using Prosopis juliflora stems as the natural carb precursor due to their abundance and providing a green and eco-friendly solution. This also provides an added benefit as Prosopis juliflora an invasive species that largely affects the ecosystem, is used for the synthesis of NCDs. The as-synthesized NCDs solution exhibited blue emission with a fluorescent quantum yield of ∼ 4.92 %. The large stoke shift indicates that the fluorescent emission may be attributed to defect states. Hence the emission modes related to C-O and C=O surface sites made a large contribution to the emission properties. The obtained NCDs have several functional groups on their surface leading to great water solubility and good quantum yield. The NCDs have shown great stability and high tolerance to ionic strengths and photobleaching. The NCDs have been used for the sensing of Fe3+ ions in the aqueous media and are shown to have high selectivity and sensitivity in a linear range of 20 – 200 µM, with a LOD of 0.593 μM. Thus, the natural carbon derived NCDs can be a potential eco-friendly nanomaterial that can be applied for future metal-ion sensing applications. In addition, the NCDs was used for fluorescence-based pH detection with linearity in the pH range of 2 to 9 and showed great antioxidant properties with ∼93.59 % radical scavenging activity.
